content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
June 27, 1989 
Donald L. Rogers, C.P.A. 
Rogers, Anderson, Malody & Scott 
Vanir Tower, Ste. 300 
290 North D Street 
San Bernardino, CA 
92401 
Dear Mr. Rogers: 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal 
Assistance 
Our File No. I-89-277 
You have requested an analysis of and authority for a 
statement contained in the April 1989 edition of the FPPC Bulletin 
that candidate-controlled committees may not make reimbursements 
to candidates and campaign staff members under the provisions of 
the Political Reform Actl / as amended by Proposition 73 in the 
June 1988 primary election. 2/ 
The Commission has primary responsibility for the impartial, 
effective administration and implementation of the Act. 
(Section 
83111.) 
The Commission may adopt rules and regulations to carry 
out the purposes and provisions of the Act (Section 83112), and is 
responsible for issuing legal opinions and advice concerning the 
provisions of the Act (Section 83114). 
In addition to contribution limitations, Proposition 73 
imposed new restrictions on candidates with regard to the 
solicitation, receipt and expenditure of campaign funds. 
Specifically, Section 85200 requires any individual who intends to 
be a candidate for elective office to file a candidate intention 
form stating his or her intention to run for a specific office 
l/Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 
18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
2/your letter states only a general question. 
Therefore, we 
consider it to be a request for informal assistance pursuant to 
Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed). 
Informal assistance does not 
provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or 
formal written advice. 
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804 .. 0807 • (916)322 .. 5660 


Donald L. Rogers 
Page 2 
prior to soliciting or receiving any contribution for the purpose 
of seeking that office. 
In addition, Section 85201 requires: 
(a) 
Upon the filing of the statement of intention 
pursuant to Section 85200, the individual shall establish one 
campa1gn contribution account at an office of a financial 
institution located in the state. 
(b) 
Upon the establishment of an account, the name of 
the financial institution, the specific location, and the 
account number shall be filed with the commission within 24 
hours. 
(c) 
All contributions or loans made to the candidate, 
to a person on behalf of the candidate, or to the candidate's 
controlled committee shall be deposited in the account. 
(d) 
Any personal funds which will be utilized to 
promote the election of the candidate shall be deposited in 
the account prior to expenditure. 
(e) 
All campaign expenditures shall be made from the 
account. 
(Emphasis added.) 
As originally passed by the voters in June 1974, the Act 
contains the following definition of the term "expenditure": 
"Expenditure" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, 
a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable 
promise to make a payment, unless it is clear from the 
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political 
purposes. 
An expenditure is made on the date the payment is 
made or on the date consideration. if any, is received. 
whichever is earlier. 
Section 82025. 
(Emphasis added.) 
Therefore, a strict reading of Section 85201(e) would not 
allow candidate-controlled committees to reimburse out-of-pocket 
expenditures because, according to Section 82025, an expenditure 
for goods or services is made by the committee at the time the 
candidate or campaign worker using personal funds receives the 
goods or services, not at the time the committee makes the 
reimbursement. 
With regard to out-of-pocket expenditures made out of the 
candidate's personal funds, the requirement in Section 85201(d) 
that all personal funds of the candidate be deposited into the 
campaign account prior to expenditure imposes additional 
restrictions on the reimbursement of expenditures by candidates. 


Donald L. Rogers 
Page 3 
However, the Commission has adopted two regulations to assist 
candidate-controlled committees in complying with Section 85201. 
First, Regulation 18524 (copy enclosed) allows candidates to 
establish one or more charge or credit card accounts for campaign 
expenditures. 
The regulation also allows candidates to establish 
a $100 petty cash fund for each campaign bank account. 
Second, Regulation 18526 allows for reimbursement of certain 
expenditures made by committee employees, volunteers, agents and 
independent contractors. 
This regulation was adopted on an 
emergency basis and went into effect on April 18, 1989. 
A 
permanent regulation was adopted at the Commission's June 5 
meeting. 
Copies of both versions are enclosed. 
In addition, at its August 1, 1989 meeting, the Commission 
will consider adoption of proposed Regulation 18526.1 (copy 
enclosed), which will allow for reimbursement of a candidate's 
personal funds used for officeholder expenses. 3 / 
Please call me at (916) 322-5662 if you have additional 
questions. 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
By: 
Carla Wardlow 
Assistant Chief, Technical 
Assistance & Analysis Division 
3/This proposed regulation was previously noticed for adoption at 
the Commission meeting on July 12, 1989. 
However, due to the 
press of other business, it will not be considered until August 1, 
1989. 


DONALD L. ROGERS. C.P.A." 
RICHARD D. ANDERSON. CPA" 
JACK C SCan: CPA 
ROGERS, ANDERSON, MAWDY & SCOTT 
CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANlS 
DENNIS H. MALODY II. CPA.' 
JAY H. ZEACHER. CP.A 
WILLIAM E REINEKING. CP.A 
ROBERT B. MEMORY. CP.A 
THOMAS V. HESS. CPA 
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL C01'lPO'R.«T·'ON,9' 
VANIR TOWER. SUITE 300 
290 NORTH "0" STREET 
SAN BERNARDINO. CALIFORNIA 92401 
"ACCOUNTANCY CORPORATION 
LEONARD P KOPP, C.PA 
GARY L SILVIUS. C.PA 
MARILYN J SELLFORS. CPA 
TERRY P SHEA. CPA 
JUUE A. DASHIELL. C PA 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 IIJII Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, California 95814 
(714) 889·0871 
(714) 824-6736 
Re: 
I nterpretation of Proposition 73 
MAr 8 
AMERICAN INSTITUTE OF 
CERTI<'IED 
ACCOUNTANTS 
CAUFORN1A SOCIETY OF 
CERTIFIED 
ACCOUNTANTS 
May 3, 1989 
In your April 1989 FPPC Bulletin on Page 10 (Question 3) you state that 
"reimbursements are not allowed by a candidate...!controlled committee" to 
campaign staff members. 
I am now seeking your assistance in explaining your position to various 
candidates which our firm represents. 
The requirement that all campaign expenditures be made from the campaign 
bank account does 
not 
seem 
inconsistent 
with 
the 
reimbursement 
for 
candidates for out-of-pocket costs if made within the reporting period. 
It 
is clearly impractical for all minor expenditures to be paid by a campaign 
account check. 
Therefore, if you have an opinion from the Attorney 
General or any other authority for your position, please provide us with a 
copy as soon as possible. 
Your timely response will be appreciated. 
Sincerely, 
Rogers, Anderson, Malody & Scott

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
May 9, 1989 
Donald L. Rogers, C.P.A. 
Rogers, Anderson, Malody & Scott 
Vanir Tower, Suite 300 
290 North "0" Street 
San Bernardino, CA 
92401 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-277 
Dear Mr. Rogers: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on May 8, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your reque~t poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.
